channel.title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Marine
channel.description: Curated OT/ICS advisories, incidents and threat intelligence. Editorial by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

item.title: [Critical] FortiBleed: ~430,000 Fortinet firewalls swept for 110M+ credentials — and a July maritime bulletin ties the stolen logins to active ransomware
item.description: CISA is pressing Fortinet operators to act after researchers assessed the FortiBleed campaign swept roughly 430,000 internet-facing FortiGate firewalls and SSL-VPN gateways — about half of those exposed worldwide — for more than 110 million credentials, and a 3 July maritime cybersecurity bulletin ties that stolen access to active INC Ransom and Lynx ransomware operations. For shipping companies and terminals the exposure is the remote-access layer: the same appliances connect shoreside offices, vessel networks and port systems, and because credentials leaked rather than a bug being exploited, there is no patch to close the gap. An attacker signing in with valid credentials looks like a legitimate mariner or IT admin, so treat any exposed device as compromised and rotate now — before it becomes the doorway to a terminal-halting ransomware event. - CISA (hardening alert) / Cydome maritime bulletin - CISA alert 18 Jun 2026; maritime bulletin 3 Jul 2026 — active exploitation

item.title: [High] Anubis ransomware on Italy's Adriatic Port Authority shows how a single port compromise ripples across a region
item.description: The Adriatic Port Authority, which oversees the Port of Ancona, was compromised in December 2025 in an attack later attributed to the Anubis ransomware gang, disrupting maritime trade across the region and forcing shipment delays and vessel rerouting. Researchers who detailed the case pointed to aging port infrastructure and thin cybersecurity practices as the enabling conditions — a profile that fits many regional ports. For terminal and port operators it is a concrete reminder that ransomware against back-office and terminal-operating systems translates directly into halted cargo movement, so segmentation, offline backups and tested recovery of the terminal operating system matter as much as perimeter defense. - Industrial Cyber / Resecurity - Compromise Dec 2025, attributed Jan 2026 — foundational context

item.title: [High] CYTUR: maritime cyber incidents jumped 103% in 2025, with ransomware now reaching shipboard OT
item.description: CYTUR's 2026 Maritime Cyber Threat report found incidents rose from 408 in 2024 to 828 in 2025 — a 103% jump — with DDoS, ransomware and malware leading. The most consequential shift for operators is OT-targeted ransomware moving from IT into critical shipboard systems including ballast-water control and engine monitoring, where an outage is a safety event, not just a business one. The report frames hyper-connected smart ships as the driver and calls for secure-by-design rather than bolt-on controls. Use it to prioritize segmentation between ship IT and OT and to pressure-test how a vessel operates if monitoring or control systems are disrupted. - CYTUR / Industrial Cyber - CYTUR 2026 report (Feb 2026) — foundational context

item.title: [High] GNSS spoofing and jamming keep degrading navigation and AIS — and it is no longer only a war-zone problem
item.description: Positioning interference continues to disrupt maritime navigation at scale, with more than a thousand vessels a day affected in some regions and vessels grounded after spoofing placed them far from their true position. The threat has also reached U.S. approaches: seven vessels near the Port of Long Beach reported GPS outages on 29 January. Because bridge navigation, AIS and increasingly automated systems lean on GNSS, spoofing and jamming are effectively an OT-integrity problem for ships and ports. Crews need trained manual and celestial fallback, and operators should treat position and timing as data that can be attacked, not trusted by default. - Industrial Cyber - Ongoing 2026; Long Beach outage 29 Jan 2026 — dated context

item.title: [High] CISA flags ST Engineering iDirect iQ-Series satellite terminals — the VSAT gear that keeps ships and ports connected
item.description: On 2 July CISA released an advisory for ST Engineering iDirect iQ-Series satellite terminals, part of a six-advisory batch. iDirect VSAT platforms are widely used for maritime satellite connectivity, so a vulnerable terminal sits on the primary link between a vessel or remote facility and shore — a foothold with reach into both communications and any connected onboard systems. Check the advisory for affected models and firmware, apply the vendor's guidance, and restrict management access to the terminal. - CISA ICS Advisories - Advisory 2 Jul 2026

item.title: [Policy] USCG Cybersecurity in the MTS rule: training deadline passed, Cyber Officer and plans due July 2027
item.description: The U.S. Coast Guard's Cybersecurity in the Marine Transportation System rule has been in effect since 16 July 2025, and its first phased obligation — cybersecurity training for personnel with IT/OT access — carried a 12 January 2026 deadline that has now passed. Next up, regulated U.S.-flagged vessels and facilities must designate a Cybersecurity Officer and submit a USCG-approved Cybersecurity Plan by 16 July 2027, backed by a cybersecurity assessment. Operators should be building toward those deliverables now, and reviewing the USCG's updated FAQs for how requirements apply to their vessels and facilities. - USCG Maritime Commons - Rule effective 16 Jul 2025; CySO/plan due 16 Jul 2027

item.title: [Policy] IACS UR E26/E27 push cyber requirements into newbuild vessel and equipment design
item.description: The classification societies' unified requirements IACS UR E26 (ship cyber resilience) and E27 (system and equipment cyber resilience) apply to newbuild vessels and set expectations for how cyber resilience is engineered into hulls and onboard systems from the keel up. For owners and yards the effect is that cybersecurity is now a design-and-class matter, not just an operational one — equipment must arrive with defined security capabilities and documentation. Confirm newbuild and major-conversion projects are specifying E26/E27 conformance and that suppliers can evidence it. - Marine Public / IACS - In force for newbuild vessels (contracts on/after 1 Jul 2024)
